mechanism_narrative: Rising nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflect strong labor demand and income growth, which boosts consumer spending and corporate earnings, leading to positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days. This signal is always active at current levels.
failure_condition: Walk-forward Rank IC is negative or below 0.03 over a 3-year rolling window, or the proportion of positive forward returns when PAYEMS increases month-over-month is not significantly above 50% (z-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate rises above 3.5%, it signals a sufficiently restrictive monetary stance that increases the discount rate on equity cash flows and slows economic activity, resulting in negative S&P 500 returns over the subsequent 20 trading days.
failure_condition: In periods where FEDFUNDS is above 3.5%, the mean 20-day forward return is not less than the unconditional mean (one-sided t-test p > 0.10), or the hit rate of negative returns is below 60%.

mechanism_narrative: Simultaneously rising retail sales (RSAFS) and housing starts (HOUST) indicate robust domestic demand that amplifies corporate revenue growth, generating positive S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days. The joint increase of both series provides a stronger signal than either alone.
candidate_trigger: Both RSAFS and HOUST increase month-over-month
regime_hint: expansionary phase with strong consumer and housing sectors
failure_condition: When both RSAFS and HOUST increase concurrently, the 20-day forward SPX return is not positive in more than 60% of cases, or the interaction effect in a regression is not statistically significant at the 10% level.

mechanism_narrative: During the monetary tightening cycle that began in mid-2004, each incremental increase in the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) reduces equity valuations by raising the risk-free rate and tightening financial conditions, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over 20 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:Federal Reserve raised rates 9 times from June 2004 to June 2005
failure_condition: Within the tightening cycle regime, the median 20-day forward return following rate increases is non-negative, or the negative prediction accuracy is less than 55% over the cycle period.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp month-over-month decline in industrial production (INDPRO) signals a contraction in manufacturing output that precedes weakening economic conditions, causing negative S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days as markets reprice recession risk.
candidate_trigger: INDPRO falls more than 0.5% in one month
failure_condition: When INDPRO declines by more than 0.5% month-over-month, the 5-day forward SPX returns are positive in more than 50% of cases, or the average return is not less than -0.5%.

mechanism_narrative: Sustained positive growth in real GDP (GDPC1) reflects a healthy expanding economy, supporting corporate earnings and equity valuations. This trend signal is always active and predicts positive S&P 500 returns over 60 trading days following a quarterly GDP release.
failure_condition: The Rank IC of GDPC1 level or growth rate versus 60-day forward returns is below 0.02 over any 5-year rolling window, or the mean forward return following positive GDP growth quarters is not positive.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate (UNRATE) is low (below 5%) and inflation (CPIAUCSL) is rising, it points to an overheating economy with potential Fed tightening that will compress equity valuations. The combination of tight labor markets and price pressures creates a negative outlook for S&P 500 returns over 20 trading days.
failure_condition: When both conditions are met, the 20-day forward SPX return is positive in more than 55% of instances, or the overlay of CPIAUCSL does not significantly improve the negative predictive power over using UNRATE alone (p > 0.10 in an interaction regression).

mechanism_narrative: During a housing boom (period of elevated HOUST), a subsequent decline in housing starts signals demand saturation and potential reversal of the housing cycle, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days as the economy's key growth driver falters.
candidate_trigger: HOUST declines more than 3% from its 12-month high
evidence_refs: fact:Housing starts peaked at 2.27 million units in January 2005 before declining
failure_condition: When HOUST falls more than 3% from a 12-month high, the 20-day forward returns are positive in at least 50% of occurrences, or the average return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p > 0.10).
